narration_text:
Think you heard God — but you’re not sure? Try these three biblical tests before you act.

A few years ago a woman I know had a clear, urgent impression to quit her job and move to a new city. At the same time she felt fear, excitement, and a small inner voice telling her to wait. She prayed, she Googled, she replayed the thought until she couldn’t tell which voice was God, which was hope, and which was simply fear. Maybe that sounds familiar. We all hit moments like that — decisions tangled with competing inner voices. The stakes feel high, and the last thing you want is to mistake wishful thinking for a word from the Lord.

Here’s the good news: God doesn’t leave us to a guessing game. The Bible gives simple, reliable tests to help us tell His voice from our own thoughts. They aren’t mystical tricks. They’re practical, biblical guardrails you can use today. The three are: Scripture alignment, peace and fruit over time, and wise community confirmation. I’ll walk you through how they work, give quick examples, point out common pitfalls, and end with a short prayer you can use right now.

First, the Scripture test. If it’s truly God speaking, it will never contradict Scripture. His voice will line up with His character and His revealed will. Scripture is the measuring rod. If an impression tells you to do something that conflicts with God’s commands — to lie, exploit someone, or neglect love and justice — it’s not from God. For example, a man felt compelled to get revenge after being wronged. The urge for “justice now” felt intense, but the Bible calls us to leave vengeance to God and to pursue reconciliation when possible. That impression failed the Scripture test.

Pitfall: emotional intensity can masquerade as divine certainty. Just because a thought feels powerful doesn’t make it biblical. Make a habit of asking, “Does this align with Scripture?” If you’re unsure, look up passages about God’s character and relevant commands. Memorize a few key verses like Philippians 4:6–7 about prayer and peace, or Galatians 5:22–23 about the fruit of the Spirit. Scripture is the foundation; if an impression stands on that ground, it’s worth further testing.

Second, the peace and fruit test. Jesus said, “My sheep hear my voice” and they know it by the Shepherd’s presence. God gives a sense of peace, not ongoing torment, and His leading produces fruit that fits His character over time. That doesn’t mean you won’t feel nervous — big steps can be scary — but the overall trajectory should show spiritual fruit: humility, love, patience, self-control, deeper dependence on God. If a decision produces growing anxiety, more selfishness, or spiritual dryness, that’s a red flag.

Example: a woman felt prompted to cut off every friend who disappointed her. At first it felt cleansing, even liberating. But after acting, she was more isolated, proud, and easily offended. The decision produced bad fruit. Sometimes we mistake relief or temporary adrenaline for God’s peace. Watch the fruit over weeks and months. God’s voice leads to life, not further spiritual decay.

Pitfall: waiting for an immediate, cinematic “peace” moment. Discernment often unfolds slowly. Give it time. Small tests and incremental steps let you observe the fruit without leaping into irreversible choices.

Third, the community confirmation test. God rarely speaks to your heart without also speaking through His people. Scripture values counsel — Proverbs calls many advisers wise, and the early church tested prophecies in community. Bring your impression to trusted, mature believers: a pastor, a mentor, or a spiritually wise friend. Share what you sense, explain how it lines up with Scripture, and listen humbly to their observations. They may bring passages you missed, point out blind spots, or confirm a clear lead.

Example: a young man felt called to ministry but was tempted to hurry and start fundraising alone. His pastor and a couple of mature friends saw practical obstacles and spiritual immaturity; their questions helped him realize he needed more training. Their counsel didn’t silence God; it refined his obedience. Community doesn’t replace God’s voice, but it helps interpret it.

Pitfall: seeking only affirmation. If you gather friends who always agree, you’ll end up in an echo chamber. Invite honest, loving critique and be willing to wait for wise consensus.

How do you put these tests into practice right now? Start with a simple prayer: “Lord, if this is from you, show me through your Word, give me your peace, and lead me to wise counsel. Don’t let me mistake my desires for your voice.” Then do the homework. Look up relevant Scripture and journal what you sense and how it aligns. Take a small, reversible step if you can and watch the fruit. Share the impression with one or two trusted believers and ask them to pray and speak honestly. If Scripture, peace/fruit, and community all point the same way, move forward with confidence. If they don’t line up, be patient — discernment often grows through waiting and refining.

Remember, discernment isn’t a single miraculous event but a skill formed over time. God is patient; He trains us like a loving parent, correcting and guiding until we learn to recognize His voice. The more you root decisions in Scripture, watch for godly fruit, and walk with wise community, the calmer and clearer your discernment will become.

If this helped, leave a comment about a time you weren’t sure whose voice you were hearing. Share this video with someone who needs clarity, and subscribe for more calm, biblical encouragement.
